About Me

Building at the Edge of Electricity

I'm Nitish Kumar, a Sydney-based Electrical Engineer holding a B.Tech in Electrical & Electronics Engineering from Maharaja Agrasen Institute of Technology (GGSIPU, Delhi) — graduated First Division with a CGPA of 8.71 — and currently completing a Master of Engineering at Western Sydney University, where I achieved a High Distinction (91) in Engineering Project 1.

Currently working as a CNC Technician at Gerry's Glass, managing three CNC machines, performing advanced fault diagnosis, and producing technical electrical drawings. I bridge the gap between mechanical precision and electrical intelligence.

Outside the workshop, I design and build ambitious personal projects — from orbital tracking systems and high-voltage plasma physics to AI-powered command centers — driven by a passion for engineering that refuses to sit still.

Featured Work

Engineering
Projects

PROJECT — 001

2-Axis
Satellite Tracker

A precision dual-axis tracking system capable of following low-earth-orbit satellites. Combines a high-torque linear actuator with a 380kg.cm servo for azimuth and elevation control. Progressed from Arduino joystick prototype to full-scale perforated dish on aluminium tripod.

Linear ActuatorServo Control Arduino UNORF SystemsPID Control
Primary ActuatorLinear Actuator
Servo Torque380 kg.cm
AxesAzimuth + Elevation
ControllerArduino UNO + Joystick
How It Works
01Dual-axis mechanical frame — elevation axis via linear actuator arm, azimuth (pan) via high-torque rotary base on MDF enclosure.
02Arduino UNO control — reads analog joystick X/Y → maps deflection to PWM signals → drives both servo channels simultaneously.
03Full-scale dish pointing — perforated aluminium dish on adjustable tripod simulates real-time antenna steering toward satellite passes.
04Power — 9V alkaline battery powers Arduino; servo current drawn from the regulated 5V rail.

PROTO V2Linear actuator elevation arm + MDF azimuth drive enclosure
PROTO V2Full assembly — dish elevated to 45° tracking angle

PROJECT — 002

High-Power
Inverter

Custom-designed square-wave inverter delivering 15V at 10A (150W) continuous. Full H-bridge topology built on perfboard with hand-wound dual toroidal inductors, red polypropylene capacitor bank, MOSFET array on aluminium heatsink with active cooling fan.

Power ElectronicsH-Bridge Gate DriversMOSFETsPWM Control
Output Voltage15V DC
Output Current10A Continuous
WaveformSquare Wave
TopologyFull H-Bridge
How It Works
01H-Bridge switching — Four MOSFETs in an H-bridge; diagonal pairs switch ON alternately, reversing current to produce a square wave.
02Gate driver ICs provide high-current gate pulses, preventing shoot-through and reducing switching losses.
03150W output — MOSFET selection and heatsinking matched to thermal dissipation requirements at 15V / 10A continuous.
04PWM frequency control — Output frequency set by 555-timer or MCU PWM, adaptable for motor drive or AC simulation.
Build Gallery High-Power Inverter — 15V / 10A Square Wave
INVERTERTop view — dual hand-wound toroidal inductors + PP capacitor bank
INVERTERAngled view — MOSFET H-bridge on aluminium heatsink with active cooling fan
Dual toroidal inductors (hand-wound)
Red polypropylene capacitor bank
MOSFET H-bridge on aluminium heatsink
Active cooling fan + perfboard construction
PROJECT — 003

Ionic
Thruster

Electrohydrodynamic propulsion device generating thrust through ionised air — zero moving parts. Powered by a custom flyback HV driver feeding a Cockcroft-Walton voltage multiplier to achieve kilovolt-range fields across twin spiked emitter electrodes.

HV Power SupplyCockcroft-Walton Voltage MultiplierEHD PropulsionHigh Voltage
HV StageCockcroft-Walton Multiplier
PropulsionElectrohydrodynamic
Moving PartsZero
MediumIonised Air
How It Works
01Cockcroft-Walton multiplier — diode-capacitor ladder steps up AC input through cascaded half-wave rectifier stages, multiplying peak voltage.
02kV field generation — intense electric field across sharp emitter electrodes ionises surrounding air molecules.
03EHD thrust — ionised molecules accelerate emitter→collector under the field, dragging neutral air → measurable thrust with zero moving parts.
04Silent propulsion — no mechanical components, completely silent operation for research into silent UAV propulsion.
Build Gallery Ionic Thruster — HV Supply + Cockcroft-Walton Multiplier
IONICHV driver board — flyback transformer + polypropylene capacitor bank
IONICPower supply unit — live dual voltmeter reading 11.8V / 0.00A
IONICCockcroft-Walton multiplier — capacitor ladder stage (front)
IONICCockcroft-Walton multiplier — HV rectifier diode stack (rear)
IONICThruster emitter array — twin cylindrical spiked ion-emission electrodes
Flyback HV driver board
Cockcroft-Walton voltage multiplier (cap + diode ladder)
Twin spiked emitter electrode array

PROJECT — 004

Solar HHO
Generator

Solar-powered oxyhydrogen generator using potassium hydroxide (KOH) electrolyte. Custom stainless steel electrode plate stack on threaded rod assembly, sealed in a clear housing with a bubbler water trap for clean HHO output.

Solar PowerElectrolysis KOH ElectrolyteClean EnergyElectrochemistry
Power SourceSolar PV
ElectrolyteKOH (Potassium Hydroxide)
Output GasHHO (Oxyhydrogen)
ApplicationRenewable Energy Research
How It Works
01Solar PV input — powers the electrolysis cell directly, fully off-grid. KOH dramatically lowers cell resistance vs pure water.
02KOH electrolyte — dissolved KOH increases conductivity, boosting gas production efficiency significantly.
03Electrolysis — DC current between SS plates splits H₂O → hydrogen at cathode, oxygen at anode → oxyhydrogen (HHO) collected.
04Bubbler water trap — gas bubbled through water trap removing electrolyte mist, producing clean HHO for research testing.
Build Gallery Solar HHO Generator — KOH Electrolysis Cell
HHOElectrolysis cell core — stainless steel electrode plate stack on threaded rod assembly
HHOFull system — electrolysis cell + KOH solution + bubbler water trap + gas output tubing
Stainless steel electrode plates (hand-cut, stacked)
KOH electrolyte solution in sealed clear housing
Bubbler / water trap for clean HHO output
Pneumatic push-fit gas plumbing
PROJECT — 005

Tesla
Coil

Resonant air-core transformer producing high-frequency HV discharges. Hand-wound secondary on PVC former, custom driver circuit with toroidal inductor and active cooling. Demonstrated wireless power transmission — two incandescent bulbs lit with zero wired connection.

Resonant CircuitsHigh Voltage RF EngineeringSpark GapWireless Power
TopologySpark Gap SSTC
OutputHigh-Voltage HF Arc
Wireless Demo✓ Proven
Capacitor BankCustom MMC Design
How It Works
01Primary LC tank — transformer charges MMC capacitor bank; at breakdown, spark gap fires and dumps energy into primary coil.
02Resonant coupling — primary and secondary tuned to same frequency; secondary voltage rises dramatically via turns ratio → streamers.
03Wireless power demo — incandescent bulbs placed near the coil light up purely from the EM resonance field — zero wired connection.
04RF discharge — streamers are HF arcs at hundreds of kHz, far too fast for electrocution but carrying sufficient energy to be hazardous.
Build Gallery Tesla Coil — Resonant HV Spark Gap Design

TESLA Secondary coil — magnet wire on PVC former
⚡ WIRELESS POWER
TESLA Two bulbs glowing wirelessly — zero wired connection, powered by EM resonance

PROJECT — 006

Jarvis
AI Command Center

A fully modular, multi-AI personal assistant — voice I/O, real-time web search, image generation, app automation, and content writing in a single Python pipeline. Three frontier AI models orchestrated by a 2-layer decision engine running on Mac Mini with ESP32 hardware bridge.

PythonLLaMA 3 70BCohere R+ Stable Diffusion XLEdge TTSSelenium STT
Intent RouterCohere command-r-plus
Chat + SearchLLaMA 3 70B via Groq
Image GenStable Diffusion XL (HF)
Automation Types8 Commands
How It Works — Full Pipeline
01Selenium STT — headless Chrome captures mic input via Web Speech API. Multi-language queries auto-translated via mtranslate.
02Cohere command-r-plus classifies intent into 12 categories (general, realtime, open, close, play, generate image, system, content, search, reminder…)
03Parallel AI routing — LLaMA 3 70B for chat; Mixtral 8x7B for content writing; SDXL fires 4 async parallel image requests with random seeds.
04asyncio.gather() dispatches 8 automation command types non-blocking. Edge TTS (+5Hz pitch, +13% rate) delivers neural voice responses.
Project Architecture Jarvis AI — 7-Module Python Pipeline
JARVIS Project file structure — Mac Mini deployment

PROJECT — 007

Light Signal
Conditioning

ELEC7008 Instrumentation & Measurement project at WSU. An LED driven by a Labradog signal generator transmits an optical signal. Two LDRs capture it alongside environmental noise. A differential amplifier cancels common-mode noise, then an active bandpass filter, passive bandpass filter, and output buffer progressively recover the clean signal across 5Hz–200Hz.

Op-Amp Differential Amp Active BPF Passive BPF Dual LDR Arduino UNO
Signal SourceLED + Labradog Generator
SensorsDual LDR Array
Noise RejectionDifferential Amplifier
Filter ChainActive BPF → Passive BPF
Output StageNon-Inverting Buffer
Frequency Range5Hz → 200Hz
How It Works — Signal Chain
01LED signal transmission — Labradog generator modulates an LED at the target frequency. The light beam carries the signal through air to two LDRs.
02Differential amplifier — LDR A0 receives signal + noise; LDR A1 receives noise only. The diff amp subtracts A1 from A0, cancelling all common-mode ambient interference.
03Active non-inverting BPF (A2) — passes only the target frequency band with controlled gain, attenuating out-of-band components.
04Passive BPF + output buffer (A3) — second filter stage removes residual noise; unity-gain buffer drives the load cleanly. A3 confirms full signal recovery vs A0 raw.
Build Gallery Light Signal Conditioning — ELEC7008 · WSU
SIGNALReal breadboard — Arduino UNO · dual LDR array · op-amp ICs · indicator LEDs
SIGNALTinkercad simulation — full op-amp signal chain · dual battery supply · Arduino UNO
A0 — Raw LDR input (signal + noise)
A1 — Reference LDR (noise only)
A2 — After differential amp + active BPF
A3 — Final clean output after passive BPF + buffer ✓
